trips.name: Bavarian Classics & Neuschwanstein
trips.description: Bavaria's greatest hits circuit combines the fairy-tale castles of Mad King Ludwig — Neuschwanstein rising from its forested crag above Füssen is the world's most visited castle — with the rococo pilgrimage church of Wieskirche and the Baroque splendour of Ottobeuren abbey. The Allgäu foothills backdrop every view with snow-capped Alps; the Forggen lake below Neuschwanstein produces the most photographed reflection in Germany.
trips.cover_search_query: Neuschwanstein Castle Bavaria Germany

trips.name: Berchtesgaden & Königssee
trips.description: The Berchtesgaden region is Bavaria's most dramatic — hemmed in on three sides by Austria and crowned by the Watzmann's sheer east face, the second-tallest wall in the Alps. Königssee is Germany's deepest and purest lake, its electric boat the only vessel allowed on its waters, echoing the famous trumpet echo off the Bartholomäkirche as it has for centuries. Hitler's Eagle's Nest tea house perches at 1,834 metres on the Kehlstein with views encompassing three countries.
trips.cover_search_query: Königssee emerald lake Bavaria Germany

trips.name: Bavarian Lake Country
trips.description: The glacial lakes south of Munich — Ammersee, Starnberger See, Tegernsee, and Chiemsee — are Bavaria's playground for Munich citizens on summer weekends, and they are genuinely beautiful: crystal-clear water, Alps as a backdrop, and Belle Époque villas of the Wittelsbach dynasty lining the shores. King Ludwig II drowned mysteriously in the Starnberger See; his memorial cross stands in the water just offshore. Chiemsee's island palace Herrenchiemsee was his most ambitious Versailles replica.
trips.cover_search_query: Chiemsee lake Alps Bavaria Germany

trips.name: German Alpine Road
trips.description: The Deutsche Alpenstraße runs 450 kilometres along the entire length of the Bavarian Alps from Lindau on Lake Constance to Berchtesgaden near Salzburg — Germany's most scenic long-distance mountain drive. It passes 20 lakes, 25 castles, several UNESCO sites, and some of the finest Baroque churches in Europe. The views of the Alps from the German side are exceptional and the driving conditions are generally more relaxed than in neighbouring Austria or Switzerland.
trips.cover_search_query: German Alpine Road Bavaria Alps landscape Germany

trips.name: Allgäu & Upper Swabia
trips.description: The Allgäu is the dairy heartland of Germany — a landscape of green hills, onion-domed Baroque churches, and the largest concentration of Baroque pilgrimage churches in the world in the Upper Swabia region just across the Baden-Württemberg border. Kempten is the oldest continuously occupied city in Germany with excellent Roman ruins; the Altusried and Ottobeuren abbeys are among the finest Baroque interiors in the country.
trips.cover_search_query: Allgäu green hills onion dome Bavaria Germany

trips.name: Bavarian Forest National Park
trips.description: The Bavarian Forest is the largest area of primeval forest in Central Europe — a vast wooded highland along the Czech border where wolves, lynx, and bison have been reintroduced. The Baumwipfelpfad treetop walk at Neuschönau is Germany's longest; the glass-blowing tradition of Zwiesel has sustained forest workshops for centuries; and the medieval Czech city of Český Krumlov makes a superb cross-border excursion over the mountain passes.
trips.cover_search_query: Bavarian Forest primeval woodland national park Germany

trips.name: Romantic Road
trips.description: Germany's most famous tourist route runs 350 kilometres from Würzburg to Füssen through medieval walled towns and Baroque churches in a journey through central European history. Rothenburg ob der Tauber is the most completely preserved medieval town in Germany — its walls are intact, its night watchman still patrols, and its half-timbered gabled houses are photographers' dream subjects. Dinkelsbühl and Nördlingen are equally perfect, equally less visited.
highlights: Rothenburg ob der Tauber medieval walls
trips.cover_search_query: Rothenburg ob der Tauber medieval town Bavaria Germany

trips.name: Munich & Day Trips
trips.description: Munich is Germany's most likeable city — an international metropolis that nevertheless maintains the beer garden, the Lederhosen, and the Weisswurst tradition with a completeness that feels authentic rather than performed. The Englischer Garten is one of the world's largest city parks; the Deutsches Museum is the world's largest science museum; and Dachau Memorial is 30 minutes away as a sobering reminder of history's darkest chapter.
trips.cover_search_query: Munich Marienplatz Frauenkirche Bavaria Germany

trips.name: Rhine Gorge Classic
trips.description: The Rhine Gorge between Bingen and Koblenz is UNESCO World Heritage — 65 kilometres of the world's most celebrated river scenery, where vineyards climb steep slate cliffs above a succession of medieval castles and wine-producing villages. The Lorelei rock, where sailors were allegedly lured to their deaths by a siren's song, rises 130 metres from the Rhine at the sharpest bend; the castles of Rheinfels, Katz, and Maus compete for the most impregnable position.
trips.cover_search_query: Rhine Gorge castles vineyards Germany

trips.name: Moselle Wine Road
trips.description: The Moselle winds in enormous loops from Trier to Koblenz through the steepest vineyards in the world — slate slopes up to 68 degrees that can only be worked by hand, producing Rieslings of extraordinary mineral precision that are among the finest white wines on earth. The wine villages of Bernkastel-Kues, Traben-Trarbach, and Cochem each have their own distinct architectural character, and every village has a Weinstube where the local growers serve their wine in the traditional narrow glasses.
trips.cover_search_query: Moselle River vineyards Bernkastel-Kues Germany

trips.name: Trier & Roman Heritage
trips.description: Trier is the oldest city in Germany — a Roman provincial capital whose surviving monuments are the most complete north of the Alps. The Porta Nigra gate, the Imperial Baths, the Amphitheatre, and the Basilica of Constantine (still in use as a Protestant church) form a UNESCO World Heritage ensemble of extraordinary completeness. Karl Marx was born in Trier in 1818, and his birthplace museum is one of the city's most visited sites.
trips.cover_search_query: Trier Porta Nigra Roman gate Germany

trips.name: Nahe & Rheingau Wine Country
trips.description: The Rheingau is Germany's most aristocratic wine region — a south-facing riverside strip of Riesling vineyards between Rüdesheim and Wiesbaden where the Cistercians of Kloster Eberbach were making wine in the 12th century and where the Schloss Johannisberg estate claims the oldest Riesling vines in Germany. The Nahe valley behind it is less famous but equally compelling, with rocky volcanic landscape and mineral Rieslings rivalling the Moselle.
trips.cover_search_query: Rheingau Riesling vineyards Rhine Germany

trips.name: Middle Rhine Castles & Rüdesheim
trips.description: The Middle Rhine between Mainz and the Loreley concentrates more hilltop castles per kilometre than anywhere else in Europe — many are medieval ruins romantically reclaimed by forest, others are 19th-century Romantic restorations built by wealthy Prussians enchanted by the landscape. The Niederwald monument above Rüdesheim, the twin castles of Sterrenberg and Liebenstein, and the impregnable Rheinfels near St Goar are the highlights of a river cruise by car.
trips.cover_search_query: Middle Rhine castles hilltop vineyards Germany

trips.name: Black Forest High Road
trips.description: The Schwarzwald-Hochstrasse runs along the northern crest of the Black Forest from Baden-Baden to Freudenstadt — a ridge road through ancient fir forests, boggy moorland plateaus, and granite summits that on clear days offer views to the Alps and the Vosges simultaneously. The resort town of Baden-Baden has the most beautiful Belle Époque thermal baths in Germany (the Friedrichsbad), and a casino that Dostoyevsky described as the ruin of his life.
trips.cover_search_query: Baden-Baden Friedrichsbad spa Black Forest Germany

trips.name: Freiburg & the Southern Black Forest
trips.description: The southern Black Forest is the highest and most dramatic part of the range — the Feldberg at 1,493 metres is the highest peak in Germany outside the Alps, and the Wutachschlucht gorge is the wildest canyon in the country. Freiburg im Breisgau is one of Germany's sunniest and most charming cities, its Gothic Minster presiding over a medieval centre of students, cyclists, and an outdoor culture reflecting the warmth of a city that sits at the foot of vineyards.
trips.cover_search_query: Freiburg im Breisgau Minster Black Forest Germany

trips.name: Triberg & Baden-Baden Classic
trips.description: The central Black Forest around Triberg is the region most visitors imagine — cuckoo clock workshops, folk costume museums, and the Triberg Waterfalls (Germany's highest accessible waterfall at 163 metres) in a landscape of deep valleys and dark fir canopy. The Kinzig valley between Triberg and Offenburg threads through a series of half-timbered towns that were important trading centres on the east-west route across the forest.
trips.cover_search_query: Triberg waterfalls Black Forest Germany

trips.name: Black Forest to Lake Constance
trips.description: The descent from the Black Forest plateau to Lake Constance (the Bodensee) passes from dense fir forest through flower meadows to one of Germany's warmest and most fertile landscapes. Lake Constance is shared between Germany, Austria, and Switzerland — a vast inland sea with the island town of Konstanz, the flower island of Mainau, and the wine island of Reichenau (Europe's largest market garden and UNESCO World Heritage Site) among its highlights.
trips.cover_search_query: Lake Constance Bodensee Mainau flower island Germany

trips.name: Dresden & Saxon Switzerland
trips.description: Dresden was the Florence of the Elbe — a Baroque city of such extravagance that Augustus the Strong rebuilt it as a statement of Saxon power, filling the Zwinger palace with porcelain and the Semperoper opera house with Beethoven's premieres. The Elbe Sandstone Mountains south of Dresden — known as Saxon Switzerland — are a bizarre landscape of needle-like rock formations and table mountains crossed by the Bastei bridge in one of Germany's most dramatic panoramas.
trips.cover_search_query: Dresden Frauenkirche Baroque Elbe Germany

trips.name: Leipzig & Central Saxony
trips.description: Leipzig punches far above its size — Bach worked here for 27 years at the Thomaskirche; Wagner was born in the city centre; and the Gewandhaus orchestra is one of the world's oldest. The 1989 Monday demonstrations in front of the Nikolaikirche that helped bring down the Berlin Wall began here, and the contemporary arts scene that followed has made Leipzig Germany's most creatively vibrant city. The Völkerschlachtdenkmal monument is Europe's largest battle memorial.
trips.cover_search_query: Leipzig Thomaskirche Bach Saxony Germany

trips.name: Thuringia: Classical Heritage
trips.description: Thuringia is Germany's cultural heartland — Weimar was Europe's greatest concentration of artistic and intellectual talent in 1800 (Goethe, Schiller, Herder, Liszt, Nietzsche all lived here) and again in 1919 when Walter Gropius founded the Bauhaus school that revolutionised design. Erfurt's medieval old town is the most complete in eastern Germany, its merchants' bridge lined with 32 inhabited medieval houses as it has been since the 14th century.
trips.cover_search_query: Weimar Goethe House classical heritage Thuringia Germany

trips.name: Wartburg & Eisenach
trips.description: The Wartburg above Eisenach is Germany's most historically resonant castle — where Martin Luther translated the New Testament into German in 1521 (inventing modern written German in the process), where the Minnesingers competed in song contests in 1206 (Wagner's Tannhäuser), and where the Landgravines of Thuringia ruled one of medieval Europe's most cultured courts. Bach was born in Eisenach below in 1685.
trips.cover_search_query: Wartburg Castle Eisenach Thuringia Germany

trips.name: Thuringian Forest
trips.description: The Thüringer Wald is Germany's most accessible mid-mountain walking region — a 150-kilometre ridge of forested hills along the Rennsteig watershed that formed the historic boundary between Thuringia and Franconia. Glass-blowing villages, porcelain workshops, and medieval trade towns line the valleys; Schmalkalden's half-timbered town centre is one of the finest in Germany, and Sonneberg was the world capital of toy making for a century.
trips.cover_search_query: Thuringian Forest Rennsteig ridge Germany

trips.name: Harz Mountains Classic
trips.description: The Harz is the highest mountain range in northern Germany — a granite upland of spruce forest, granite tors, and medieval silver-mining towns that stands in dramatic contrast to the flat plains of Lower Saxony surrounding it. The Brocken at 1,141 metres is the highest summit and has a legendary association with Walpurgisnacht witches' sabbath — Goethe's Faust includes a scene set here. Goslar's medieval old town and its imperial palace are UNESCO World Heritage Sites.
trips.cover_search_query: Goslar medieval imperial palace Harz Germany

trips.name: Harz Narrow-Gauge Railways
trips.description: The Harz has the most extensive narrow-gauge steam railway network still operating in Germany — three lines totalling 140 kilometres that cross the mountains from Nordhausen to Wernigerode via the Brocken summit. The Brockenbahn to the summit is particularly dramatic in winter. The network was built in the 1890s and survived the GDR era through sheer economic usefulness — it now carries both tourists and a genuine freight purpose.
trips.cover_search_query: Harz narrow gauge steam railway Brocken Germany

trips.name: Quedlinburg & the Romanesque Road
trips.description: Quedlinburg is one of Germany's most important medieval cities — a UNESCO World Heritage town of 1,300 half-timbered houses spanning 900 years of construction, crowned by a Romanesque collegiate church where Heinrich I and Otto I are buried. The Romanesque Road between Naumburg and Goslar passes some of the finest 11th-12th century religious architecture in Europe, including the cathedrals of Halberstadt and Naumburg.
trips.cover_search_query: Quedlinburg half-timbered Romanesque Harz Germany

trips.name: Hamburg & Lübeck Hanseatic
trips.description: Hamburg and Lübeck were the two dominant cities of the Hanseatic League — the medieval trading confederation that controlled Baltic commerce for three centuries. Hamburg's red-brick Speicherstadt warehouse district (UNESCO) and its hyperactive Reeperbahn create a city of contrasts; Lübeck's Holstentor is Germany's most recognisable medieval gate and its old town is a UNESCO island of Gothic red-brick that includes the house where Thomas Mann was born.
trips.cover_search_query: Lübeck Holstentor Hanseatic old town Germany

trips.name: Rügen Island & Baltic Coast
trips.description: Rügen is Germany's largest island — a landscape of white chalk cliffs, beech-forested capes, and Jugendstil resort towns built in the early 20th century when German aristocracy took the healing air of the Ostsee. The Jasmund National Park's Königsstuhl chalk cliffs — the 'King's Chair' — were painted by Caspar David Friedrich in 1818 in one of German Romanticism's most famous images. The massive unfinished KDF resort at Prora is Europe's longest building.
trips.cover_search_query: Rügen chalk cliffs Königsstuhl Baltic Germany

trips.name: Schleswig-Holstein & Flensburg Fjord
trips.description: Schleswig-Holstein is Germany's most Scandinavian state — wedged between the North Sea and the Baltic, with a character strongly influenced by Danish culture (and Danish territory until 1864). The Schlei fjord cuts deep inland; Schleswig's cathedral has the finest Gothic triptych in northern Europe; and the Flensburg fjord at the Danish border is a landscape of small harbours, yacht clubs, and rum distilleries that seem to belong more to Copenhagen than Berlin.
trips.cover_search_query: Flensburg fjord Schleswig-Holstein Germany

trips.name: Mecklenburg Lake District
trips.description: The Mecklenburg Lake District is Germany's largest water landscape — a glacially formed plateau of more than 1,000 lakes connected by rivers and canals where houseboats and kayaks are as common as cars. Schwerin's storybook ducal castle sits on an island in the Schweriner See; Güstrow's castle has the finest Renaissance interior in northern Germany; and Waren an der Müritz is the gateway to the Müritz National Park.
trips.cover_search_query: Schwerin castle island lake Mecklenburg Germany

trips.name: Nuremberg & Franconian Heritage
trips.description: Nuremberg is Germany's medieval showpiece and its most troubling modern monument simultaneously — the Kaiserburg fortress dominates a superbly reconstructed Gothic city that was the 'unofficial capital' of the Holy Roman Empire, while the Nazi Rally Grounds and the Nuremberg Trials courthouse make it essential for understanding 20th-century history. Bamberg to the north is UNESCO-listed for its intact medieval city and its own unique smoked beer.
highlights: Bamberg UNESCO old town smoked beer
trips.cover_search_query: Nuremberg Kaiserburg castle old town Franconia Germany

trips.name: Franconian Switzerland
trips.description: Franconian Switzerland (Fränkische Schweiz) is a landscape of limestone cliffs, deep valleys, and castle ruins that packs 1,000 years of medieval history into a compact area of northern Bavaria. The concentration of castles per square kilometre is the highest in Germany; the breweries per capita are arguably the highest in the world — more than 200 breweries serve a region of 300,000 people. Pottenstein's castle and the Teufelshöhle cave are among the finest natural and historic sites in northern Bavaria.
trips.cover_search_query: Franconian Switzerland limestone cliff castle Bavaria Germany

trips.name: Altmühltal Nature Park
trips.description: The Altmühl valley is one of the most scenic river valleys in Bavaria — a lazy limestone river cutting through white Jurassic cliffs in a landscape that has yielded Archaeopteryx fossils, Ice Age tools, and the largest collection of Roman limes frontier remains in Germany. The valley is ideal for cycling between medieval towns; Eichstätt is a Baroque city of extraordinary quality for its size; and Pappenheim's dual castle above the river is one of the most romantic ruins in Bavaria.
trips.cover_search_query: Altmühltal river valley limestone cliff Franconia Germany

trips.name: Heidelberg & the Odenwald
trips.description: Heidelberg is Germany's most romantic city — the ruined Schloss above the Neckar river inspired the German Romantic movement, and the university (founded 1386) still fills the cafes of the Altstadt with students as it has for six centuries. The Odenwald hills behind the city are a landscape of forested ridges, half-timbered villages, and Nibelungenlied legend where Siegfried was killed by Hagen at the Odenwald spring. Michelstadt's medieval market place is among the finest in Germany.
trips.cover_search_query: Heidelberg castle ruins Neckar Romantic Germany

trips.name: Swabian Alb
trips.description: The Swabian Alb is a limestone plateau of deep valleys, castle ruins, and the headwaters of the Danube — Germany's most underrated scenic region. The Danube Gorge between Beuron and Sigmaringen is one of the most spectacular river valley drives in Germany; Lichtenstein Castle was the model for Walt Disney's first vision of a castle; and the Uracher Wasserfall is one of Germany's finest natural waterfalls in a beech-forested landscape.
trips.cover_search_query: Swabian Alb Danube Gorge limestone Beuron Germany

trips.name: Lake Constance Circuit
trips.description: The 273-kilometre circuit of Lake Constance passes through Germany, Austria, and Switzerland in one of Europe's most pleasant lake drives. The German shore combines the perfectly preserved medieval city of Überlingen, the casino and Belle Époque architecture of Konstanz, and the colourful painted houses of Lindau's island old town with views of the Austrian Alps that seem implausibly close across the clear water.
trips.cover_search_query: Lindau island Lake Constance Bavaria Germany

trips.name: Stuttgart & Swabian Wine
trips.description: Stuttgart is Germany's engineering capital — Porsche and Mercedes-Benz both have outstanding museums here — in a city unusually set among vine-covered hills that descend into the Neckar valley. The Württemberg wine region surrounding the city produces the most diverse wines in Germany from 14 different grape varieties; the Württembergisch Unterland between Heilbronn and Stuttgart has the most concentrated wine-growing of any German metropolitan area.
trips.cover_search_query: Stuttgart Porsche Museum Swabian wine Germany

trips.name: Berlin & Potsdam
trips.description: Berlin is Europe's most compelling capital — a city of shattering 20th-century history where the Wall's course can still be walked, where the Reichstag's glass dome offers the most politically charged panorama in Europe, and where more museums per capita than any other city in the world occupy palaces, warehouses, and island complexes. Potsdam 30 minutes away has Frederick the Great's Sanssouci as the finest Prussian Rococo landscape in Germany.
trips.cover_search_query: Berlin Brandenburger Tor Reichstag Germany

trips.name: Spreewald Biosphere
trips.description: The Spreewald is one of Germany's most unusual landscapes — a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve where the Spree river divides into 200 channels threading through alder forest, creating an inland delta where Sorbian (Slavic) culture has survived since the 6th century. Traditional flat-bottomed Kahn punts are still the primary transport between the villages; the Lübben and Lübbenau canal towns are the starting points for paddle journeys through an aquatic forest unlike anywhere else in central Europe.
trips.cover_search_query: Spreewald canal Kahn punt Brandenburg Germany

trips.name: Havel Lakes & Brandenburg an der Havel
trips.description: The Havel river west of Berlin widens into a chain of lakes — the Schwielowsee, Templiner See, and Werbellin See — that form one of Germany's finest inland waterscapes, beloved by Berliners with weekend houses in the reedy lakeside forests. Brandenburg an der Havel is a Romanesque and Gothic city of surprising quality on a island between the river branches; Rheinsberg's early 18th-century palace is where Frederick the Great spent his happiest years.
trips.cover_search_query: Rheinsberg palace lake Brandenburg Germany

trips.name: Eifel Volcanic Lakes & Aachen
trips.description: The Eifel plateau is Germany's volcanic surprise — the Vulkaneifel contains the only active volcanic field in central Europe outside Iceland, with maar lakes (circular lakes in volcanic craters) of extraordinary clarity at Daun, Manderscheid, and Hillesheim. Aachen was Charlemagne's capital and its octagonal cathedral is Germany's oldest UNESCO World Heritage Site — the throne on which 30 German kings were crowned for 600 years still stands in the Palatine Chapel. The Eifel National Park completes a region of remarkable diversity within an hour of the Netherlands and Belgium.
trips.cover_search_query: Aachen Cathedral Charlemagne Eifel Germany
